expert_comments.text:
I'm putting this here but it concerns both [728] and [729].

So I've been working on what the "general" statement is, following Bloom's suggestion. Here's one.

There exist absolute constants $c_1, c_2 > 0$ with the following properties. Let $f: \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$ be any function with $f(M) \le \exp(c_1 \sqrt{\log M})$ for all $M$ large. Let $\varepsilon > 0$ be arbitrary. Then for all sufficiently large $M$, there is $m \in [M, (1+\varepsilon)M]$ such that
$$ v_p((2m)!) + v_p(f(M)!) - v_p(m!) - v_p((m+f(M))!) \ge c_2 \frac{\log M}{\log p} \cdot [p \le 2k] $$
for all primes $p$.

(That's a lot of quantifiers!)

To get [728]. You take $f(M) = \lfloor c \log M \rfloor$. You want $LHS \ge 0$. This is easy: $\log M/\log p > 0$ anyway.

To get [729]. You still take $f(M) = \lfloor c \log M \rfloor$. But now you want $c_2 \log M/\log p \ge v_p(f(M)!)$ for all primes $p \ge p_\min(c)$. Now $v_p(f(M)!) \sim c \log M/(p-1)$. Hence this holds for $p$ large, with threshold depending only

expert_comments.author: natso26
expert_comments.text:
Follow up: the LHS there is perhaps more intuitive as
$$ v_p(\binom{2m}{m}) - v_p(\binom{m+f(M)}{m}) \ge c_2 \frac{\log M}{\log p} \cdot [p \le 2k]. $$
Also, "there is $m \in [M, (1+\varepsilon)M]$" can be upgraded to "for all $m \in [M, (1+\varepsilon)M]$ with $o(M)$ exceptions".

We can also get Pomerance's result: the set of $n$ with
$$ (n+1)(n+2)\dots (n+k) | \binom{2n}{n} $$
has asymptotic density $1$, for fixed $k$ or "$k$ that grows slowly" (not explicitly worked out by Pomerance).

In our language, with $k=f(M)$, we again want $c_2 \log M/\log p \ge v_p(f(M)!)$. (The difference with [729] is now we want all primes, not just large primes.) This works out to be $f(M) \le c \log M$ for some (small) constant $c > 0$. So Pomerance's result holds with $k \le c \log n$ for some $c > 0$.

In conclusion: this formulation unifies at least 3 results, [728], [729], and Pomerance's, each with slightly different arguments!

expert_comments.author: natso26
expert_comments.text:
Second follow up: from the formulation in the Pomerance's latest note, I see that this can be cleaned up further as follows. (Still using essentially the same argument.)

There exist absolute constants $c_1, c_2 > 0$ with the following properties. Consider the set $S$ of $m \in \mathbb{N}$ such that, for all $0 \le k \le  \exp(c_1 \sqrt{\log m})$,
$$ v_p(\binom{2m}{m}) - v_p(\binom{m+k}{m}) \ge c_2 \frac{\log m}{\log p} \cdot [p \le 2k] $$
for all primes $p$. Then $S$ has asymptotic density $1$.

expert_comments.author: Kevin Barreto
expert_comments.text:
Hmm, I’m not familiar with Lean, but it could be that some things are simply hard to formalize in Lean (but you need someone with more experience to look at it and see how to help Aristotle here). Alternatively, the proof may really contains flaws that we haven’t caught; or Aristotle is simply “bad” at this particular proof for whatever reason.

I think there are also 2 ways to proceed here. You can choose whichever one:

1) Get a proof with maximum AI autonomy. Which means you might not want to start from my ChatGPT conversation, because the more human interaction there is, the more complex the workflow becomes and the more you could argue that we need humans to “unblock” the process.

2) Get the best proof with maximum human-AI collaboration. Which means we do any tinkering that we think will help carry the process to the finish line.

Actually given that we want to (scientifically) test out AI autonomy, I kind of want to go with 1) for now… but your choice. But if we really seem to

I gave GPT-5.2 Pro Aristotle's prior Lean formalisation attempt, and asked it to add the remaining lemma statements, sorried and with provided solution sketches in the format shown here. It seems to have allowed Aristotle to be making more progress and is still currently trying to fill in all the sorries, but it hasn't terminated quickly like before.

To be clear, this is still technically all AI-generated; my only involvement was in telling GPT-5.2 Pro to lay some of the Lean file foundations for Aristotle. Hopefully I should have a formalisation back from it within a few hours.

But yeah, I’m definitely trying to go end-to-end on 1) for scientific purposes. I’m sure I could probably manually formalise things if I tried. If all f

expert_comments.author: natso26

The issue now seems to be in this "exists_good_m_medium_primes" lemma, for which Aristotle is unable to find a proof for. I am currently trying to look into what is going on there. It is likely that GPT-5.2 misformalised the statement as opposed to there being an actual issue in the TeX informal proof.

expert_comments.author: Kevin Barreto
expert_comments.text: The informal proof (or something very close to it) is definitely correct. What Aristotle produced seems to almost be correct. The main issue seems to be it took the threshold $t_p$ twice as large as in the informal proof and that caused problems. GPT-5.2 Pro set it up correctly there. I wonder if there is something we're missing that spooked Aristotle into making that change. It's possible there's something wrong with the threshold in the informal version, but I don't see it. Apparently $\lceil 10 \log \log M\rceil$ works in any case.

expert_comments.author: Kevin Barreto
expert_comments.text:
I ran a ChatGPT Deep research query.  One new relevant reference was located: a paper of Ulas and Schinzel from 2013, numerically exploring a large number of related binomial coefficient divisibility problems raised by Erdos-Straus and Erdos-Graham (including #376, #389, and #396).  However, strangely enough, neither #728 or #729 are mentioned in this paper.  So while it does appear there is no prior literature on these problems, this is more an indication that these problems have somehow been neglected by the literature, as opposed to having been studied without significant progress.

EDIT: On rereading [ErGr80] I came across problem #401, which appears to be Erdos and Graham's revised version of the problems #728, #729 (which comes from the earlier paper [EGRS75]): compare in particular the "If we disregard the small primes then the situation probably changes" from [ErGr80, p. 78] with the "it is possible that this is due only to the small primes" from [EGRS75, p. 90].  Perhaps this

expert_comments.author: TerenceTao
expert_comments.text:
I would expect that (it would be very strange otherwise).

If a problem has been significantly studied and still open, it must be hard. I don't expect AI to solve hard problems yet, so more likely: 1) the problem is not significantly studied, or 2) the problem is not open (has prior literature).

expert_comments.author: natso26
expert_comments.text: Congrats 🎉!

expert_comments.author: natso26
expert_comments.text: Thanks. Now we have to wait to see if it passes the KoishiChan test.

expert_comments.author: Kevin Barreto
expert_comments.text:
I have done several rounds of literature searches in the past few days, examining every paper that google scholar and mathscinet linked to [ERGS75] and [ErGr80]. I also examined every paper of Erdos with binomial coefficient or factorial in the title.

So far nothing has turned up beyond what Tao has found. However I can think of several remaining possibilities: 1) PhD thesis or books, 2) notes uploaded to personal websites that never got published and 3) problems in magazines as in the original a! b! | n! problem.

I also know one instance where an Erdos problem was solved in a paper that does not cite the original paper. Not sure if this can be the case here. I suggest sending an email to Carl Pomerance and see if he knows anything. If anyone can reach Rusza, might worth checking with him as well.

expert_comments.author: KoishiChan
expert_comments.text: I asked Carl about this, will update if I hear back.

expert_comments.author: TerenceTao
expert_comments.text:
I heard back from Carl.  He confirmed that "it seems fairly easy to follow the ideas in my 2015 paper to
prove that for infinitely many $n$ there is a value of $k = \exp((\log n)^{1/2+o(1)})$ with $\binom{n+k}{k} | \binom{2n}{n}$", by using the lemma $\nu_p(\binom{n+k}{k}) \leq \max \{ \nu_p(n+i): 0 < i \leq k\}$ which he imagined would be known to Erdos, but was not aware of any followup work where this observation was already made in print.  This is essentially also the argument provided by GPT. So it appears that the solutions to #728, #729 are technically new to the literature, but would have been well within reach of experts if they had devoted any non-trivial attention to the questions.

expert_comments.author: TerenceTao
expert_comments.text: Yep, this was my expectation. 728 and 729 seemed definitely in reach of Pomerance, say, but just no one bothered to push on it. At least it is neat that the AI systems are beginning to be able to construct such arguments, though.

expert_comments.author: Kevin Barreto
expert_comments.text:
I think this 728/729 case is interesting in the sense that the argument, when written up, appears “presentable” in and of itself. In contrast, for 205, which I also just deformalized, the content does not appear interesting at the level of publishable material, even though that’s also new.

So, to think about these things early, there seems to be many levels of mathematics contributions:

1) Routine, not new
2) New, not that interesting <- I believe most AI contributions will fall here currently.
3) Interesting, but not hard (for experts) <- seems rare currently, but we have one example.
4) Hard, but doable with effort <- this looks like something to watch out; a contribution at this level is probably near regular human-mathematician level.
5) More than that <- speculative; I can’t properly imagine what this will be like yet, but we should be aware that the possibility exists.

expert_comments.author: natso26
expert_comments.text:
Oh, while I was going through the GPT-5.2 Pro's writeup (which is not that easy to follow) to determine whether it's correct, I instead found a proof by adapting the argument in my writeup in [728].

I thought it's valuable to record it here, even if Aristotle succeeds later.

So in GPT-5.2 Pro's writeup, we are reduced to: $W_p(m) \le \kappa_p(m)$ for every $K(C) \le p \le 2k$. The part up to here is correct. (We must show such $K := K(C)$ exists for any $C > 0$.)

Now in my [728]'s writeup, we are reduced to: $V_p(m) \le \kappa_p(m)$ for every $p \le 2k$. Difference: no lower bound for $p$, and change $W$ to $V$.

In GPT-5.2 Pro's writeup, there is a bound $W_p(m) \le k/(p-1) + V_p(m)$ which I think is correct (there's also a similar bound in my [728]'s writeup).

So to adapt, we must accommodate the extra $k/(p-1)$ thing. Now we're not going to touch the (more complicated) probabilistic argument, but we're zooming in on the Lemma 7 (threshold inequality). This is part of the final c

https://gemini.google.com/share/c95e39d6949a

expert_comments.author: old-bielefelder
expert_comments.text:
If I may suggest, there are certain "hidden assumptions" in the way you interact with Gemini which makes its output less reliable.

For example, the first prompt: "read the pdf carefully and list strengths and weaknesses. strict math mode." quietly *assumes* that the pdf is correct. Gemini will think it's correct and will not consider that it could be incorrect.

Moreover, you explicitly asked "Can you formulate the problem and your examples in a way that it may serve as exercise in a bachelor exam in math?" which assumes that such an exercise is suitable (it may not be!). Then you asked later: "Would this topic (Erdos 729) suit for a master thesis in math (for candidates who might also want to go a PhD track)?". This latter question cannot get a good answer at all because you already use "bachelor exam" as a baseline!
